text: He looked like a sheep with a secret sorrow.
She looked at me as if I were some sort of strange insect that had crawled out from under a flat stone.
The laughs, which had started well, faded like a bugle call in a distant wood.
It is never difficult to distinguish between a Scotsman with a grievance and a ray of sunshine.
She fitted into my biggest armchair as if it had been built round her by someone who knew they were wearing armchairs tight about the hips that season.

I'm not absolutely certain of my facts, but I rather fancy it's Shakespeare who says that it's always just when a fellow is feeling particularly braced with things in general that Fate sneaks up behind him with the bit of lead piping.
setup: Bertie, briefly at peace with the world, on the nature of his luck:

text: There is only one cure for grey hair. It was invented by a Frenchman. It is called the guillotine.
setup: On the subject of ageing gracefully:

text: It is a good rule in life never to apologize. The right sort of people do not want apologies, and the wrong sort take a mean advantage of them.
setup: On the wisdom of apologies:

text: The voice of Love seemed to call to me, but it was a wrong number.
setup: Bertie, reflecting on a romantic entanglement that was not going as hoped:

text: Jeeves, I have an idea.
setup: On being asked to resolve a situation entirely of his own making:

dialogue_response.text: I would advise against it, sir.

text: I don't know if you have had the same experience, but one of the drawbacks of being engaged to one girl while you are in love with another is that conversations with the former are apt to become a little difficult.
setup: Bertie, on the complications of his current romantic situation:

dialogue_response.text: If I might suggest, sir, a brief period of silence would serve you admirably at this juncture.

text: Aunt Agatha is not a woman who is lightly thwarted.
Lord Emsworth's attention had been riveted, from the very first moment, on the Empress of Blandings. She was looking even more rotund and beautiful than ever.
Comrade, I am with you. Let us go and baffle them.
My Aunt Agatha, the one who chews broken bottles and kills rats with her teeth.
setup: On Aunts:

text: I was so darned sorry for poor old Corky that I hadn't the heart to touch my breakfast. I told Jeeves to drink it himself.
setup: On sympathy:

text: It was one of those cases where you approve the broad, general principle of an idea but can't help being a bit doubtful about how it will work out in practice.
setup: On Plans:

text: I always advise people never to give advice.
setup: PG Wodehouse, on the subject of advice:

text: There is no surer foundation for a beautiful friendship than a mutual taste in literature.
setup: On the basis of true friendship:

text: Uncle Fred was a man who went about the world scattering light-heartedness as a dog scatters fleas.
setup: On Frederick Altamont Cornwallis Twistleton, 5th Earl of Ickenham:

text: He looked as if he had been poured into his clothes and had forgotten to say 'when'.
She entered the room in the manner of one who has just received a nasty letter from the income-tax authorities.
He moved through the crowd with the easy grace of a carthorse making its way through a field of standing corn.
She looked at me the way my Aunt Agatha looks at a caterpillar she has found in her head of lettuce.
He wore the expression of a man who has just discovered that a loved one has been at the whisky.
She sagged. Not much, but perceptibly — like a leaky tyre.
He sprang to his feet with the agility of a man of sixty-five who has been stung by a wasp.
Never put anything on paper, my boy, and never trust a man with a small black moustache.
setup: Bertie, passing on the sum total of his uncle's worldly wisdom:

text: You can't be a successful Dictator and design women's hats. One or the other. Both is out.
setup: On Roderick Spode and his side venture into fashion:

text: Success comes to a writer, as a rule, so gradually that it is always something of a shock to him to look back and realize the heights to which he has climbed.
setup: PG Wodehouse, reflecting on a career in letters:

text: She was a girl who was more or less moulded on the lines of Cleopatra, and personally I have always thought that if Cleopatra had been a bit taller and her hair a bit darker she might have looked rather like Corky.
setup: Bertie, attempting to describe a female acquaintance:

text: Bertie, you are an ass.
setup: After Bertie presents what he considers a perfectly sound scheme:

text: Jeeves, what would I do without you?
setup: Bertie, emerging unscathed from yet another entanglement:

dialogue_response.text: It is to be hoped, sir, that the occasion for such a contingency will not arise.

text: Bingo, old fruit, you can't keep falling in love with a different girl every two weeks.
setup: Bertie, attempting to counsel his oldest friend Bingo Little:

dialogue_response.text: I know, Bertie. And yet it keeps happening.

text: Gussie Fink-Nottle was a man who devoted his life to the study of the newt in all its facets and ramifications.
Aunt Dahlia is the one decent aunt left to me — a large, hearty woman who hunts and shoots and is a bit free with the language but fundamentally sound.

Madeleine Bassett held the view that the stars were God's daisy chain, and cried when she saw a dead kitten. I had never met anyone like her before, and felt I could readily get on without meeting anyone like her again.
It is a curious thing about engagements. They come upon you suddenly, and before you know it you are in the soup.
setup: On becoming engaged, again:

text: I turned to Aunt Agatha, whose demeanour was now rather like that of one who, picking daisies on the railway track, has just caught the down express in the small of the back.
setup: When a scheme, for once, does not go as Aunt Agatha planned:

text: The only reason I went to school was to improve my mind, but I am bound to confess that the process took a good deal of time.
setup: Bertie, on the formative years:

text: He had the look of one who has drunk the cup of life and found a dead beetle at the bottom.
She uttered a sound rather like an elephant taking its foot out of a mud hole in a Burmese teak forest.
Like so many substantial Americans, he had married young and kept on marrying, springing from blonde to blonde like the chamois of the Alps leaping from crag to crag.
I could see that, if not actually disgruntled, he was far from being gruntled.
setup: Bertie, assessing the emotional weather at Totleigh Towers:

text: Into the face of the young man who sat on the terrace of the Hotel Magnifique at Cannes there had crept a look of furtive shame, the shifty, hangdog look which announces that an Englishman is about to talk French.
setup: In one of the most celebrated opening sentences in English comic fiction:

text: My Uncle George discovered that alcohol was a food well in advance of modern medical thought.
setup: Bertie, on his family's pioneering contribution to nutritional science:

text: The trouble with you, Spode, is that just because you have succeeded in inducing a handful of halfwits to disfigure the London scene by going about in black shorts, you think you're someone.
setup: Bertie, deploying Jeeves's secret weapon against Roderick Spode:

text: Jeeves, what do ties matter at a time like this?
setup: On whether sartorial standards may be relaxed during a crisis:

dialogue_response.text: There is no time, sir, at which ties do not matter.

text: You know, Jeeves, you're a genius.
setup: Bertie, after yet another last-minute deliverance:

dialogue_response.text: I would not go so far as to say that, sir. I have certain gifts.

text: Bertie, you are a fathead. An incredible ass.
setup: Aunt Agatha, delivering her annual assessment of Bertie's character:

text: Jeeves, I want you to approach this matter with an open mind.
setup: Bertie, softening up the resistance before presenting his plan:

dialogue_response.text: I shall endeavour to do so, sir, though I confess the effort presents certain difficulties.

text: Jeeves, I have a feeling that Fate is against me.
setup: Bertie, mid-crisis, seeking some form of moral support:

dialogue_response.text: It would perhaps be more accurate to say, sir, that the situation requires careful handling.

text: Aunt Dahlia is the one aunt I can put up with. She hunts — or did when younger — and the effect of a life in the saddle has given her a carrying sort of voice that can be heard from a considerable distance.
Gussie Fink-Nottle is a man who, having devoted his life to the study of the newt, has not always found the social graces coming naturally.
Tuppy Glossop is the sort of chap who, given a delicate situation, can be relied upon to select the most injurious course of action available and pursue it with enthusiasm.
Lord Emsworth is at his best in the company of pigs. Among human beings he achieves only a sort of wandering contentment; with the Empress of Blandings at his side he attains true serenity.
It is no use telling me there are bad aunts and good aunts. At the core they are all alike. Sooner or later, out pops the cloven hoof.
setup: On the fundamental nature of aunts:

text: The fascination of shooting as a sport depends almost wholly on whether you are at the right or wrong end of the gun.
setup: On the matter of field sports:

text: Jeeves is always right. This is not a conclusion I have reached lightly. It is the fruit of long and frequently painful experience.
setup: Bertie, reflecting on his valet with the wisdom of hard-won experience:

text: I am not a man who gives his heart readily, but, once given, you can rely on Bertram Wooster to see the thing through if he has to do it on his hands and knees through broken glass.
setup: Bertie, on the depth of his personal loyalty:

text: I could see that Fate had been getting the short end of it from the start and was in no mood to make allowances for human frailty.
setup: Bertie, surveying the wreckage of his latest scheme:

text: I just sit at a typewriter and curse a bit.
setup: Wodehouse, asked to describe his writing process:

text: An author who expects results from a first novel is in a position of a man who drops a rose petal down the Grand Canyon of Arizona and listens for the echo.
setup: Wodehouse, on the expectations appropriate to a debut novelist:

text: Every author really wants to have letters printed in the papers. Unable to make the grade, he drops down a rung of the ladder and writes novels.
setup: Wodehouse, on the novelist's position in the literary pecking order:

text: I believe there are two ways of writing novels. One is mine, making a sort of musical comedy without music and ignoring real life altogether; the other is going right deep down into life and not caring a damn.
setup: Wodehouse, on the two schools of novel-writing and which camp he occupies:

text: I have found in life that a little inexactitude saves a world of explanation.
setup: The Hon. Galahad Threepwood, on the practical advantages of imprecision:
